title: Delta of Venus
description: Delta of Venus is a collection of erotic short stories by Anaïs Nin. Restricted 16 in 1979.
descriptionParagraphs: Delta of Venus is a collection of erotic short stories by Anaïs Nin. Restricted 16 in 1979.

Its interest lies partly in the way literary or informational writing gets collapsed into a public-morality problem. As a collection of erotic short stories, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in New Zealand. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Restricted 16 in 1979. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Delta of Venus is a collection of erotic short stories by Anaïs Nin. Restricted 16 in 1979.
banningParagraphs: Delta of Venus entered censorship debates as a collection of erotic short stories associated with morality, print scandal, and sexuality. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around obscenity and public morality.
The earliest event currently captured here is 1979 in New Zealand, where Indecent Publications Tribunal classified, prohibited, or restricted. Restricted 16 in 1979. Restricted 16 in 1979.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: Literature Suppressed on Sexual Grounds
counterReadings.author: Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: Surveys the legal and moral language used to suppress books as obscene.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for seeing how obscenity law and censorship habits changed over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
